times for travel in China August offers hot sticky weather and many attractions will be busy whilst the winter is quieter but temperatures do dip we recommend visiting in the winter if you are on a tight budget because hotel prices are lower but make sure to bring layers to keep warm so now let's look at getting there many choose to fly into Shanghai directly especially if you are using the city as a stopover on your way to another destination in Asia there are direct flights between Shanghai and a few cities in Europe including London whilst plenty of stopover flights connect Shanghai to North America China also has an extensive train Network connecting Shanghai to many cities and towns in the country by a speed train book tickets at the train station or use the c-trip app to book tickets in advance we will leave the link in the description below so now let's look at getting around once you're in Shanghai you have a few options for getting around the Metro is one of the busiest Subway systems in the world with 19 lines connecting you to the whole city this is the quickest and easiest way to get around Shanghai the routes are color-coded and you can download the Shanghai Metro app to help you plan your Journeys Shanghai also has has an extensive bus system that connects you to the suburbs of the city and this is the cheapest way to travel in Shanghai you can also use the taxis or use the app Diddy which is similar to Uber we don't recommend using these during rush hour as traffic can be bad at this time but generally taxis are affordable and easy to use especially when using the Diddy app Shanghai also runs a few different bike sharing apps ideal for those visiting in the warmer months so now let's look at typical prices Shanghai is a great place for Budget Travelers dorm beds can be found from 140 Chinese Yuan well it's budget rooms in good locations begin at 400. mid-range rooms begin at 650 plus more luxurious options begin at around 800. local breakfast can be found from between 10 to 15 what's a western breakfast in a cafe costs around 40 Yuan dishes in local restaurants range from 20 to 80 per dish with hot pots typically costing around 100 per person so China is a great place to visit if you are traveling on a budget and there are a few ways that you can save money in Shanghai firstly save money on accommodation by not only booking videos.transcript: Welcome to Shanghai, the beating heart of modern China! Shanghai beautifully blends traditional charm with modern sophistication. I just got back from an epic trip, and I'm stoked to share my ultimate 3-day itinerary. Get ready for the best sights, delicious eats, and insider tips on how to make the most of your time AND your money. Day 1: Experiencing the Best of Shanghai's Skylines and Shopping For the first day, you can start with exploring the heart of Shanghai's modern and historical landmarks. Lujiazui (陆家嘴) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] The first stop is Lujiazui. Lujiazui, often dubbed the "Wall Street of China," is a prominent financial district located in the Pudong area of Shanghai. Board Line 2 or Line 14 and alight at Lujiazui Station, where you will see the iconic Oriental Pearl TV Tower, as well as some of the world's tallest skyscrapers: the Shanghai Tower, the Shanghai World Financial Center, and Jin Mao Tower For panoramic views of the city and Huangpu River, spend 200 RMB to visit the observation deck in the Oriental Pearl TV Tower. (introduce a longer footage) Alternatively, visit the highest bookstore in the world, Duo Yun Bookstore, located on the 52nd floor of the Shanghai World Financial Center. Enjoy a cup of coffee for 32 RMB while taking in the stunning views of the Huangpu River. If you’re not afraid of heights, you can walk on the glass skywalk at Jin Mao Tower 88th floor, which is 340 meters above the ground. Set aside around 3-4 hours to explore the Lujiazui area. Ferry [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] After enjoying the sky-high views, head to the river. Take a 10-minute walk to Dong Chang Road Pier, and for just 2 RMB, board the ferry. While it can have long queues, it offers a similar experience to river cruises but at a fraction of the cost. Nanjing Road Pedestrian Street (南京路步行街) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] After the ferry ride, you will reach The Bund. From there, take a walk to Nanjing Road Pedestrian Street, one of the world's busiest shopping streets and a symbol of the city's vibrant urban life. There are many Instagram-worthy spots along the road, along with famous eateries with high reputations and long histories. Enjoy noodles at Shen Da Cheng, one of the best local Chinese restaurants on Nanjing Road. The food is delicious, comes in large portions, and is reasonably priced. Personally, I love shopping at Shanghai First Foodhall, founded in 1921. It offers excellent quality at affordable prices. The Bund (外滩) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] Save the best for last. When night falls, it’s time to head back to The Bund, another of Shanghai's must-visit spots. Stroll along the 1.5-kilometer-long promenade and experience the incredible contrast between the historical colonial buildings on one side and the futuristic skyscrapers of Pudong on the other. The street will be immersed in a pool of lights, creating a magical atmosphere. You can also enjoy the views of Lujiazui across the river. The modern skyline of Pudong steals the show with its dazzling array of lights. The skyscrapers, including the Oriental Pearl Tower, the Shanghai World Financial Center, and the Shanghai Tower, are lit with vibrant, colorful LED displays that often change colors and patterns. The reflection of these lights in the Huangpu River adds an extra layer of magic to the scene, making a walk along The Bund at night a truly unforgettable experience. It is a great place to take a walk and enjoy the night scenes. Day2: Exploring Traditional and Modern Shanghai Second day is for you to experience the perfect blend of Shanghai's historical charm and contemporary vibrancy as you explore its iconic gardens, bustling streets, and dynamic nightlife. YuYuan Garden (豫园) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] Start your day with a visit to the beautiful YuYuan Garden. If you're using the metro, you can take Line 10 or Line 14 and alight at YuYuan Station. This classic Chinese garden, located in the Old City of Shanghai, showcases traditional Ming Dynasty architecture, serene ponds, rockeries, and lush greenery. Take your time strolling through the garden's pavilions, bridges, and pathways. The ticket price is 40 RMB, and you should set aside around 2 hours to fully enjoy the experience. YuYuan Bazaar (豫园老街) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] After your morning stroll in Yu Garden, it's time to satisfy your hunger with some delicious Chinese food. Just outside Yu Garden, YuYuan Bazaar offers a variety of shops selling jade jewelry, silk clothes, antiques, and fun souvenirs. There's plenty of delicious street food, cozy teahouses, and restaurants serving up tasty Shanghainese dishes like xiaolongbao. One of my personal favorites is Da Hu Chun. Every time I visit, I make sure to stop by this historic restaurant, established in 1932. Da Hu Chun is famous for its signature sheng jian bao, pan-fried pork buns with a crispy bottom and juicy filling – a must-try for any visitor. Another popular restaurant is Nanxiang Steamed Bun restaurant, famous for its xiao long bao. City God's Temple (城隍庙) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] After lunch, head over to the City God's Temple, located adjacent to YuYuan Garden. This Taoist temple is dedicated to the spirits of Shanghai's protectors and is a bustling hub of local worship and activity. Explore the temple's courtyards and halls, and perhaps light some incense as you experience the spiritual side of the city. Don't miss the vibrant bazaar around the temple, where you can shop for souvenirs, local snacks, and traditional crafts. The temple grounds are free to enter, but some specific halls or exhibitions may charge a small fee (around 10-20 CNY). Set aside around 1.5-2 hours for your visit. Xin Tian Di (新天地) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] In the evening, head to XinTianDi, one of Shanghai’s most popular entertainment and nightlife districts. Xin Tian Di is fairly close to Yu Garden. Take the Metro Line 10, two stops from Yu Yuan Station, you will reach Xin Tian Di Station. This area combines traditional Shikumen architecture with modern amenities, creating a unique blend of old and new. Stroll through the pedestrian streets lined with stylish boutiques, cafes, restaurants, and bars. Visit the Site of the First National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party, a significant historical landmark located in the area. XinTianDi is especially famous for its nightlife. After dinner, enjoy the vibrant scene by visiting one of the chic bars or lounges. XinTianDi offers a variety of options, from cozy wine bars to lively nightclubs, ensuring a fun and memorable evening Day 3: Discovering Historic Sites and Cultural Gems in Shanghai Today is for you to immerse yourself in the rich history and diverse culture of Shanghai. Jing’an Temple (静安寺) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] Start your day with a visit to the historic Jing’an Temple. If you're using the metro, you can take Line 2, Line 7, or Line 14 and alight at Jing’an Temple Station. This ancient Buddhist temple, located in the heart of Shanghai, dates back to 247 AD, during the Three Kingdoms period. It features grand halls, intricate statues, and serene courtyards. The temple houses the largest sitting jade Buddha statue in China. Spend time exploring the beautiful architecture and the peaceful atmosphere. The ticket price is approximately 50 RMB, and you should set aside around 2 hours to fully enjoy the experience. People’s Square (人民广场) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] After exploring Jing’an Temple, head to People’s Square. Take the metro Line 2, and alight at People’s Square Station after two stops. People’s Square is a major landmark in Shanghai, featuring expansive green spaces and impressive buildings. Visit the Shanghai Museum, which offers a vast collection of ancient Chinese art, including bronzes, ceramics, and calligraphy. Enjoy lunch at one of the many restaurants around People’s Square, with a variety of options from local eateries to international cuisines. Set aside around 2 hours for your visit. The Shanghai Museum opens free to the public, but requires online reservation through Wechat. So make sure you book online before you go. TianZiFang (田子坊) [ edit out of VO, put words for the video] In the afternoon, make your way to TianZiFang. Return to People’s Square Station, take Line 8, and alight at Lu Jia Bang Road Station after three stops. TianZiFang, located in the French Concession area, is a trendy arts and crafts enclave filled with narrow alleys, unique boutiques, art galleries, and cozy cafes. Wander through the charming streets, shop for handmade crafts, and enjoy the vibrant atmosphere. Take a break at one of the many cafes or street food vendors and sample local snacks and drinks. Set aside around 2- 3 hours to explore TianZiFang. If you can set aside more in Shanghai, there are some additional places you can visit. ZhuJiajiao (朱家角） Ready for a hidden gem? Zhujiajiao is it! This adorable water town is like stepping back in time, with winding canals and stunning old buildings. It's an easy trip on Metro Line 17, and you'll feel worlds away as you wander along ancient canals and admire Ming and Qing dynasty architecture. Enjoy a traditional lunch by the water and let the peaceful atmosphere wash over you. With its rich history and picturesque scenery, ZhuJiaJiao is a perfect getaway to experience the quieter side of Shanghai. Shanghai Disney Theme Park If you're a Disney fan, consider dedicating a full day to Shanghai Disneyland. It's a hit with locals, so be prepared for crowds even on weekdays. The new Zootopia land has only added to its popularity, making it a must-visit for many. Price for one adult is around 400 RMB. And there you have it – a little taste of what Shanghai has to offer. This city is truly a treasure trove of experiences, with something to delight every traveler. I hope this video inspires you to come and discover its magic for yourself. Thanks so much for watching! Your support means the world to me. Don't forget to give this video a thumbs up if you enjoyed it, and let me know in the comments what else you'd love to see from Shanghai. Until next time, happy travels!
